content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To : Opinion Request Meeting Participants                    Date : Jan. 7, 1902


From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
      Judy Davis

Subject: Follow-up to Opinion Request Meeting of November 19, 1981.
         Income to a Business Entity Located Outside the Jurisdiction
         from a Source Doing Business in the Jurisdiction.


ISSUES

     1. Disqualification: Did Sam have to disqualify him-
self from participating in a decision materially affecting
his business entity's sole customer?

     Ted Prim brought out that in considering a draft regu-
lation some years ago, the Commission (by a vote of 2-3)
was not at that time willing to take the position that if
a foreseeable material financial effect on a public official's
financial interest could be shown to result from a decision
by the official, there would be an "automatic" 87100 violation,
irrespective of whether or not the financial interest was
required to be disclosed. In other words, there presently
may be a conclusive presumption that if an interest need not
be disclosed, it can not be affected by a decision. Under
this analysis Sam would have to disqualify himself from
participating in the decision affecting his business entity's
customer only if Sam's sole income to his business entity from that
customer or the name of that customer were required to be
disclosed. However, language in the Commission's recent
Opinion in Overstreet, 6 FPPC Ops. 12 at 16-17, suggests
that although a financial interest may not be disclosable
(there, interest in a short-term lease), a public official
may nevertheless have to disqualify himself where a decision
he makes has a forseeable material financial effect, distinc-
guishable from the effect on public generally, on that
clearly nondisclosable interest. I read Overstreet to mean,
at the least, that whatever presumption exists against finding
an 87100 violation where the interest affected is not required
to be disclosed, that presumption is no longer conclusive.

Opinion Request
January 7, 1982
Page two

2. Disclosure

a. Did Sam have to disclose the loan from the bank? The consensus was that where a bank is located right over the jurisdictional line in an adjoining jurisdiction, it can be presumed to do business in the jurisdiction, and that therefore Sam's $50,000 loan should have been disclosed. However, it was further agreed that both Form 721, Schedule E, and the 721 Manual should be revised to make absolutely clear that loans from any source doing business or planning to do business in the jurisdiction (to be disclosed (the present form language uses "in the jurisdiction").

b. Did Sam have to disclose his pro-rata share (100%) of income from his business entity? The consensus was that where the business entity does business in the jurisdiction (Case 1 - keeps its corporate records, books, etc. within the jurisdiction) the pro-rata share of income is disclosable. As an earlier opinion request meeting had concluded that it is the filer's pro-rata share of gross income that is reportable, Sam should have disclosed on both his 1979 (when the business had a net loss) and his 1980 (when the business had a net profit) statements his pro-rata share of the gross income of his business entity. However, it was further agreed that both Form 721, Schedule D and the 721 Manual be clarified to indicate that income is disclosable where the filer's business entity "does business" or "plans to do business" in the jurisdiction (the present language reads "in the jurisdiction").

As to Case 2 (all business entity records, etc., kept outside the jurisdiction), the consensus was that in 1979, when the filer's business entity had no customers from within the jurisdiction, his pro-rata share of income was not disclosable. There was no clear consensus as to whether or not the filer's pro-rata share of 1980 income (in which year the business entity's sole customer, which provided the entity with over $10,000 in gross receipts, itself did business in the jurisdiction) was disclosable where the business entity arguably was located entirely outside the jurisdiction. One possible analysis is that even where the filer's business entity has no physical presence in the jurisdiction, but has a customer or customers who are located within the jurisdiction, the business entity can be presumed to do business in the jurisdiction by virtue of its dealings with that customer, especially where the customer as here, is the sole source of over $10,000 in income to the business entity. Perhaps the line of reasoning developed by courts in determining whether a business entity has had sufficient "minimum contacts" with a jurisdiction (thereby subjecting the entity to the courts of that jurisdiction without violating due process) could be used to determine whether or not a filer's business entity "does business" in his jurisdiction even though it may be

Opinion Request
January 7, 1981
Page three


physically located elsewhere. Under such a "minimum contacts"
analysis, Sam's business entity would be "doing business" in
Sam's county if, for example, any of the milk it sold Secureway ended up in Urban County markets.

C. Did Sam have to disclose Secureway's name as
a source of $10,000 + in gross receipts to his business entity?
The consensus was that it is clear under the Act that Sam
had to do so in Case 1, where he clearly had to disclose
income from his business entity because that entity did business in the jurisdiction. Where the filer's business entity
arguably does not do business in the jurisdiction (Case 2),
and income from it is therefore not reportable, the result
is less clear. The advice now being given is that even where
the filer's pro-rata share of income from his business entity
is not required to be disclosed, the name of any customer
doing business in the jurisdiction and providing over $10,000
in gross receipts to the business entity is nonetheless reportable. The wording of the Act ("When income of a business
entity...is required to be reported under this article, the
statement shall contain...(3)...the name of every person from
whom the business entity received payments if the filer's
pro-rata share of gross receipts from such person was...
$10,000...") implies that the filer need not disclose the
customer's name unless he is required to report his pro-rata
share of income from his business entity. Again, a possible
analysis might be that any business entity receiving over
$10,000 from a source within the jurisdiction can be presumed
to itself be doing business in the jurisdiction by virtue of
its dealings with that customer. Another analysis is that
the phrasing of §203(b) - "Income of an individual also includes a pro-rata share of any income of any business entity...
in which the individual...owns...a 10% interest or greater..."
-creates an automatic "flow through" situation, the gross receipts from the over $10,000 customer within the jurisdiction
becoming part of the individual's income.

In any event, it was determined that both the Form 721,
Schedule H, and the 721 Manual should be revised to make clear
to the filer that the names of customers or clients "doing
business" in the jurisdiction (not just "in the jurisdiction")
must be disclosed.

d. Did Sam have to disclose his investment in his
business entity? Under Case 1, Sam must disclose his investment. The forms are clear, as is the Act. Under Case 2,
he need not do so unless it can be shown that by virtue of
dealings with customers within the jurisdiction, Moo-Juice
does business there and Sam's investment in the company is
therefore disclosable.

JD:er

sections.question_synthetic: The memo addresses four disclosure and disqualification issues: (1) whether a public official must disqualify from decisions affecting their business's sole customer; (2) whether a loan from an out-of-jurisdiction bank must be disclosed; (3) whether pro-rata share of business income must be disclosed when the business is located outside the jurisdiction; and (4) whether customer names must be disclosed.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: FPPC staff concluded that: (1) disqualification may be required even where the affected interest is not disclosable; (2) the $50,000 loan should have been disclosed as the bank does business in the jurisdiction; (3) pro-rata share of income is disclosable where the business entity does business in the jurisdiction, with the 'minimum contacts' test potentially applying to out-of-jurisdiction entities with jurisdiction customers; and (4) customer names providing $10,000+ in gross receipts must be disclosed even if income itself is not reportable, though this interpretation was debated.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is internal FPPC memorandum to meeting participants rather than a formal advice letter response. Contains detailed discussion of legal issues and consensus positions but lacks traditional QUESTION and CONCLUSION sections. No specific requestor identified. Some ambiguity remains in final positions, particularly on Case 2 customer name disclosure.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: The memo addresses four disclosure and disqualification issues: (1) whether a public official must disqualify from decisions affecting their business's sole customer; (2) whether a loan from an out-of-jurisdiction bank must be disclosed; (3) whether pro-rata share of business income must be disclosed when the business is located outside the jurisdiction; and (4) whether customer names must be disclosed.

CONCLUSION: FPPC staff concluded that: (1) disqualification may be required even where the affected interest is not disclosable; (2) the $50,000 loan should have been disclosed as the bank does business in the jurisdiction; (3) pro-rata share of income is disclosable where the business entity does business in the jurisdiction, with the 'minimum contacts' test potentially applying to out-of-jurisdiction entities with jurisdiction customers; and (4) customer names providing $10,000+ in gross receipts must be disclosed even if income itself is not reportable, though this interpretation was debated.

embedding.summary: This FPPC internal memorandum from January 1982 summarizes conclusions from an opinion request meeting regarding disclosure obligations and disqualification requirements for a public official with interests in a business entity located outside the jurisdiction but doing business with an in-jurisdiction customer. Staff recommended form revisions to clarify disclosure requirements.
